data.reasoningText:
The user wants to enable hot reload (dotnet watch) for their test project so it's always enabled from the command line without setting an environment variable each time.

For `dotnet test`, hot reload is done via `dotnet watch test`. But the user mentioned not wanting to set an environment variable every time, which suggests they might be referring to `DOTNET_WATCH_RESTART_ON_RUDE_EDIT` or the `DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER` or something similar. 

Actually, for .NET test projects using `dotnet watch test`, hot reload should work. But there's also a newer approach with `dotnet test --hotreload` or similar.


I think the user is looking for a way to configure hot reload behavior persistently in the project file itself, rather than relying on command-line flags or environment variables each time. In .NET 9, you can set properties like `<DotNetWatchHotReload>true</DotNetWatchHotReload>` directly in the project file to make this the default behavior. For the Microsoft Testing Platform specifically, I can configure hot reload persistence through a `.runsettings` file or by adding properties directly to the project file, which would be cleaner than relying on environment variables or CLI flags each time. I'm realizing the simplest approach is to use the `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable or a configuration file like `testingplatform.config.json` to persist the hot reload setting, rather than passing `--hotreload` each time. For `dotnet watch test`, hot reload is already enabled by default—the user just needs to run that command and it'll automatically re-run tests when files change.
